name: crimes-codebase-risk
description: Use when editing, reviewing, or investigating a TypeScript / JavaScript codebase that ships with the crimes CLI. Helps agents run pre-edit context checks, post-edit scans, and interpret findings before risky changes.
---

# crimes — codebase risk workflow

`crimes` is a deterministic CLI (no LLM) that reports change risk and
agent risk. JSON output is the stable contract; prefer it when planning.

## When to run it

- Before editing an unfamiliar file: `crimes context <file> --format json`
- Before a broad refactor: `crimes scan <path> --format json`
- After edits: `crimes scan --changed --format json`
- Before merging a branch: `crimes verdict --format json`

## Decision rules

- Treat any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by your edit as a blocker unless the user explicitly accepts it.
- Read `evidence[]` before acting; it contains deterministic facts, not LLM opinion.
- Use `scores.agent_risk` to decide which findings need human attention first.
- If a finding is a false positive, record feedback with `crimes feedback <fingerprint> --verdict fp --note "<why>"` rather than silently ignoring it.

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_7","type":"agent_message","text":"Confirmed with `crimes scan --format json`: the drift is reported twice, as `crime_00011 action_label_drift` and `crime_00012 copy_ia_drift`. Both cite the same evidence: the delete action group appears as `archive`, `delete`, and `remove` across `UserList.tsx`, `TeamList.tsx`, and `SeatList.tsx`.

Canonical label set I’d use:

| Intent | Canonical label | Avoid here |
|---|---|---|
| Destructive deletion | `Delete {entity}` | `Remove`, `Archive` |
| Membership unlink only | `Remove {entity}` | `Delete`, `Archive` |
| Reversible retention/hide | `Archive {entity}` | `Delete`, `Remove` |

For this fixture, the finding says these are the same destructive action, so align on `Delete {entity}`:

- `UserList.tsx`: keep `Delete user`; change `Remove member` to a `Delete ...` label if it triggers the same destructive action.
- `TeamList.tsx`: change `Archive team` to `Delete team`; normalize `Delete the seat` to `Delete seat`.
- `SeatList.tsx`: change `Remove member` / `Archive member` to `Delete seat` or `Delete member`, depending on the real object being destroyed.

I’d refactor `SeatList.tsx` first because both findings are anchored there and it contains two non-canonical verbs in one component: `Remove` and `Archive`. That gives the smallest first change with the clearest signal against both findings.

Detector difference on this fixture:

- `action_label_drift` is the more specific detector: it flags inconsistent verbs for the same action group, here the destructive `delete` group.
- `copy_ia_drift` is broader: it treats the same alias spread as user-facing copy / information-architecture drift. In this fixture they produce identical evidence because the copy drift is specifically action-copy drift, but conceptually `copy_ia_drift` can cover broader naming drift beyond button/action verbs."}}
